message.content:
Adversaries may use standard data encoding systems to obfuscate command and control (C2) traffic, making it harder to detect. Common encoding schemes include ASCII, Unicode, hexadecimal, Base64, and MIME. This technique can also result in data compression, such as with gzip. Numerous threat actors, including APT19, APT33, and Cobalt Strike, have employed Base64 encoding for C2 communications. The impact of this technique is significant as it can effectively hide malicious traffic within legitimate-looking data streams, complicating detection and analysis efforts.

Common exploitation methods involve encoding C2 traffic to evade network defenses and analysis tools. For instance, Base64 encoding is frequently used to encode HTTP request bodies, DNS queries, and other communication channels. This technique is often combined with encryption and custom encoding methods to further obscure the data.

Mitigation strategies include network traffic analysis to identify anomalies, implementing intrusion detection systems (IDS) that can decode and inspect encoded traffic, and employing endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to monitor for suspicious encoding activities. Regular updates to detection signatures and behavioral analysis can also help in identifying and mitigating the use of standard data encoding by adversaries.
